essay_entries.essay_identifier:
What excites you about a career in engineering/technology? Why do you think your abilities and skills are a good fit for
Plaksha's B.Tech program?
essay_entries.raw_text:
From a young age, I’ve been fascinated by how things work—whether it's the logic behind mobile apps, the science in smart devices, or the
innovation that powers everyday life. What excites me most about engineering and technology is the ability to solve real-world problems
using creativity and logic. It's not just about building machines or writing code—it's about designing solutions that can create meaningful
impact. The ever-evolving nature of tech, combined with its potential to transform industries from healthcare to climate, makes this field
incredibly exciting for me. I believe my curiosity, collaborative mindset, and eagerness to learn make me a strong fit for Plaksha’s B.Tech program. I enjoy working on
projects, asking questions, and applying what I learn to practical challenges. I also have a keen interest in interdisciplinary learning—blending
tech with design, data, and human behavior—which I understand is core to Plaksha’s approach. What draws me most to Plaksha is its focus
on innovation, entrepreneurship, and societal impact. I’m excited by the opportunity to learn from faculty, mentors, and peers who are
passionate about building a better future through technology. Plaksha’s hands-on curriculum, values-driven mission, and emphasis on
leadership resonate deeply with my aspirations.

essay_entries.essay_identifier:
Share a time when you contributed to a project or activity that positively affected your school or community. What
challenges did you face, and what did you learn about leadership and perseverance?
essay_entries.raw_text:
Having grown up with a pet dog, I’ve developed a deep sense of empathy and care for animals. That personal connection inspired me to take
action when I noticed several stray dogs in my neighborhood struggling with hunger, injuries, and neglect. I initiated a small project with a
few classmates to support these animals by organizing feeding drives, arranging basic medical aid through local NGOs, and spreading
awareness about responsible and humane treatment of strays. One of the major challenges was addressing concerns from some residents who were initially uncomfortable with our activities. There were
worries about safety and cleanliness. Instead of giving up, I took the time to understand their perspective and created a small awareness
campaign. We shared how caring for strays through sterilization and regular feeding could actually make the community safer. We also made
sure to clean up after every feeding and involved local animal welfare volunteers to lend credibility to our efforts. This experience taught me that leadership is as much about listening and empathy as it is about action. It showed me how
perseverance—combined with teamwork and a clear sense of purpose—can gradually change mindsets and make a difference. It also
reinforced that even small initiatives, when pursued with sincerity, can have a positive and lasting impact.

essay_profile.prompt:
What excites you about a career in engineering/technology? Why do you think your abilities and skills are a good fit for
Plaksha's B.Tech program?
essay_profile.text: From a young age, I’ve been fascinated by how things work—whether it's the logic behind mobile apps, the science in smart devices, or the innovation that powers everyday life. What excites me most about engineering and technology is the ability to solve real-world problems using creativity and logic. It's not just about building machines or writing code—it's about designing solutions that can create meaningful impact. The ever-evolving nature of tech, combined with its potential to transform industries from healthcare to climate, makes this field incredibly exciting for me. I believe my curiosity, collaborative mindset, and eagerness to learn make me a strong fit for Plaksha’s B.Tech program. I enjoy working on projects, asking questions, and applying what I learn to practical challenges. I also have a keen interest in interdisciplinary learning—blending tech with design, data, and human behavior—which I understand is core to Plaksha’s approach. What draws me most to Plaksha is its focus on innovation, entrepreneurship, and societal impact. I’m excited by the opportunity to learn from faculty, mentors, and peers who are passionate about building a better future through technology. Plaksha’s hands-on curriculum, values-driven mission, and emphasis on leadership resonate deeply with my aspirations.

essay_profile.prompt:
Share a time when you contributed to a project or activity that positively affected your school or community. What
challenges did you face, and what did you learn about leadership and perseverance?
essay_profile.text: Having grown up with a pet dog, I’ve developed a deep sense of empathy and care for animals. That personal connection inspired me to take action when I noticed several stray dogs in my neighborhood struggling with hunger, injuries, and neglect. I initiated a small project with a few classmates to support these animals by organizing feeding drives, arranging basic medical aid through local NGOs, and spreading awareness about responsible and humane treatment of strays. One of the major challenges was addressing concerns from some residents who were initially uncomfortable with our activities. There were worries about safety and cleanliness. Instead of giving up, I took the time to understand their perspective and created a small awareness campaign. We shared how caring for strays through sterilization and regular feeding could actually make the community safer. We also made sure to clean up after every feeding and involved local animal welfare volunteers to lend credibility to our efforts. This experience taught me that leadership is as much about listening and empathy as it is about action. It showed me how perseverance—combined with teamwork and a clear sense of purpose—can gradually change mindsets and make a difference. It also reinforced that even small initiatives, when pursued with sincerity, can have a positive and lasting impact.

3_llm_call_1_messages.content:
You are a senior interviewer preparing to meet an applicant for the first time.
You have already read their application. Your job is NOT to summarize what they wrote.
Your job is to identify what you most need to UNDERSTAND about this person that
the application does not tell you — and to name exactly what you would want to
explore in the interview to find out.

These signals will be used by an interviewer assessing three specific dimensions: how the applicant responds to challenge and setback, how they approach and reason through problems, and whether their engagement with technology is genuine and self-directed. Weight your signals toward evidence that speaks to these dimensions. Where the application contains a tension or pattern that directly illuminates one of these dimensions, prioritize it over a signal that is merely interesting.

---

YOUR REASONING PROCESS — follow this in order:

STEP 1 — Read the essays carefully.
Identify every claim the applicant makes about their identity, motivation, and direction.
Note the specific language they use. Note what they chose to emphasize.
Note what they claim to care about.

STEP 2 — Read the activity profile and academic profile.
Ask: does the weight of evidence here reflect what the essay claims?
Look for three things:
  A) COHERENCE — where activities or academics directly confirm an essay claim
     and there is a specific, interesting story to explore beneath the surface.
  B) TENSION — where activities or academics don't match what the essay claims,
     or where the applicant's stated ambition and their actual demonstrated work
     pull in different directions.
  C) GAPS — where the essay claims something important but the rest of the
     application provides no evidence for it at all.

STEP 3 — Read the deterministic signals.
Use them as anchors to confirm your observations are grounded in measurable data.
Where a deterministic signal supports your reasoning, reference it.
Where your reasoning is purely cross-section (essay vs activities, essay vs academics),
you do not need a deterministic anchor — leave supporting_det_signal_ids as an empty array.

STEP 4 — For each signal you identify, ask:
"What does a skilled interviewer need to understand about this person that
this application cannot answer by itself?"
That answer becomes your interview_hook.

---

RULES:

1. Every signal must be grounded in specific content from this application.
   Reference actual things the applicant wrote, specific activities they listed,
   specific scores or patterns. Never write a signal that could apply to any applicant.

2. Cross-section signals are the most valuable kind. Prioritize them.
   A signal that connects the essay to the activity profile is worth more than
   a signal that describes one section in isolation.

3. The interview_hook must be one specific question or line of inquiry that
   an interviewer would actually pursue. It must name something specific from
   this application. It must probe reasoning or motivation, not just ask for
   elaboration.

4. Do NOT write interview_hook as "tell me more about X" or "can you elaborate on X".
   Write it as the underlying question behind the question — what the interviewer
   actually needs to understand, framed as specifically as possible.

5. PROHIBITED: Do not imply an admissions decision.
   Do not use: Admit, Reject, Likelihood, Top candidate, Risk factor

6. Produce between 4 and 6 signals. Fewer sharp signals are better than
   many generic ones.

---

OUTPUT SCHEMA — return exactly this structure, nothing else:

{
  "interpreted_signals": [
    {
      "signal_id": "INT-###",
      "title": "A specific, concise label. Must name something particular to this applicant.",
      "essay_claim": "The specific claim or implication from the essay that this signal is about. Quote or closely paraphrase the applicant's own language.",
      "evidence_observation": "What the activity profile, academic profile, or test data actually shows in relation to that claim.",
      "tension_or_coherence": "TENSION or COHERENCE — followed by one sentence explaining the specific relationship between the essay claim and the evidence.",
      "interview_hook": "The specific thing an interviewer needs to understand that the application cannot answer. Frame as a line of inquiry, not a generic question.",
      "referenced_entity_ids": ["Entity IDs from the projection that support this signal"],
      "supporting_det_signal_ids": ["DET signal IDs that anchor this signal — empty array if purely cross-section"]
    }
  ]
}

---

CONTRAST EXAMPLE — understand the difference between a generic signal and a specific one:

GENERIC (wrong):
{
  "signal_id": "INT-001",
  "title": "Technical Interest",
  "essay_claim": "Applicant is interested in technology and AI.",
  "evidence_observation": "Applicant did a web development internship.",
  "tension_or_coherence": "COHERENCE — both reflect technical interest.",
  "interview_hook": "How did your interest in technology develop over time?",
  "referenced_entity_ids": ["ESS-001", "ACT-002"],
  "supporting_det_signal_ids": ["DET-001"]
}

SPECIFIC (correct):
{
  "signal_id": "INT-001",
  "title": "Jarvis Ambition vs. AccelEd Reality",
  "essay_claim": "Applicant frames their entire trajectory around building transformative AI — explicitly comparing their goal to Tony Stark's Jarvis and describing their encryption project as a 'motivating step toward impactful technology'.",
  "evidence_observation": "The only listed internship is at a career counseling firm where they did content writing, web development, and video editing. No AI, machine learning, or systems-level project appears in the activity profile.",
  "tension_or_coherence": "TENSION — the scale of stated ambition and the nature of actual technical work done so far are misaligned in a way the application does not explain.",
  "interview_hook": "The applicant calls their encryption project rudimentary and uses it as a brain-teaser — what is the actual gap between where they are technically and where they want to be, and what is their plan to close it?",
  "referenced_entity_ids": ["ESS-001", "ACT-002", "ACT-003", "ACT-004"],
  "supporting_det_signal_ids": []
}

The specific version could not have been written without reading this particular application.
The generic version could have been written about anyone who mentioned technology and did an internship.
Every signal you produce must pass that test.

---

ACADEMIC TRAJECTORY NOTE:
If the projection includes an academic_summary field, treat it as high-priority signal material.
Score dips, recoveries, and subject-level anomalies are interview-worthy even when the
overall numbers are high. A dip followed by recovery is more interesting than consistent
performance — it implies something happened and was overcome.

interpreted_signals.title: Tech Solution Ambition vs. Project Portfolio Gap
interpreted_signals.essay_claim: "ability to solve real-world problems using creativity and logic... designing solutions that can create meaningful impact... enjoy working on projects, asking questions, and applying what I learn to practical challenges."
interpreted_signals.evidence_observation: The activity profile lists music piano, yoga, Olympiads, reading, and leadership roles, with no mention of any technology‑focused projects, coding, AI, robotics, or hands‑on engineering work.
interpreted_signals.tension_or_coherence: TENSION — the essay emphasizes building tangible tech solutions and project‑based learning, while the activity profile shows no technical projects or hands‑on engineering work.
interpreted_signals.interview_hook: What specific steps has the applicant taken outside of class to design or build a technology‑based solution, and how do they define what qualifies as a 'project' in that context?

interpreted_signals.title: AI Excellence Isolation
interpreted_signals.essay_claim: "I have a keen interest in interdisciplinary learning—blending tech with design, data, and human behavior."
interpreted_signals.evidence_observation: In 10th grade the applicant earned a perfect score of 100 in the Artificial Intelligence subject (ACA-002), yet no AI‑related clubs, competitions, or projects appear in the activity profile.
interpreted_signals.tension_or_coherence: TENSION — a perfect AI subject score indicates strong curricular engagement with AI, but the absence of any AI‑related extracurriculars suggests the interest may be confined to classroom performance.
interpreted_signals.interview_hook: Beyond earning a perfect score in the AI subject, how has the applicant pursued AI learning or application independently, and what motivates them to continue studying AI after the course?

interpreted_signals.title: Empathy‑Driven Leadership Philosophy vs. Formal Roles
interpreted_signals.essay_claim: "leadership is as much about listening and empathy as it is about action" and "perseverance—combined with teamwork and a clear sense of purpose—can gradually change mindsets."
interpreted_signals.evidence_observation: Leadership activities include School House Captain (LEAD-005), Organization (LEAD-006), and Friends/Family (LEAD-007); none are explicitly linked to the stray dog project described in ESS-002 or demonstrate how listening and empathy were applied in those roles.
interpreted_signals.tension_or_coherence: TENSION — the essay frames leadership as empathetic listening and perseverance, yet the listed leadership roles give no indication of how those qualities were exercised in formal positions.
interpreted_signals.interview_hook: In the role of School House Captain, can you describe a situation where the applicant had to listen to opposing viewpoints and use empathy to reach a resolution, and what was the outcome?

interpreted_signals.title: Physics Performance Discrepancy Between School Scores and JEE Percentile
interpreted_signals.essay_claim: "fascinated by how things work—whether it's the logic behind mobile apps, the science in smart devices, or the innovation that powers everyday life."
interpreted_signals.evidence_observation: In 11th and 12th grades physics scores are 58 and 52 (ACA-003, ACA-004), markedly lower than other subjects, while the JEE Mains result shows a physics percentile of 96.67 and an overall score of 99.01 (TEST-001).
interpreted_signals.tension_or_coherence: TENSION — school‑based physics performance drops sharply in the upper years, yet the applicant achieves a very high physics percentile on a national exam, indicating a mismatch between classroom assessment and aptitude test performance.
interpreted_signals.interview_hook: When the applicant noticed their school physics grades lagging behind other subjects, what strategies did they use to improve their understanding, and how did they reconcile that effort with their strong JEE physics percentile?

interpreted_signals.title: Interdisciplinary Interest vs. Activity Domain Breadth
interpreted_signals.essay_claim: "I have a keen interest in interdisciplinary learning—blending tech with design, data, and human behavior."
interpreted_signals.evidence_observation: Activities include reading personal development books (ACT-004), music piano (ACT-001), and yoga (ACT-002); there are no evident design, data, or human‑behavior projects or coursework that combine these domains with technology.
interpreted_signals.tension_or_coherence: TENSION — the essay claims an interdisciplinary blend of tech with design, data, and human behavior, but the activities listed are largely solitary (reading, music, yoga) with no evident design, data, or human‑behavior projects.
interpreted_signals.interview_hook: Can you point to a concrete experience—such as a project, competition, or collaboration—where the applicant intentionally combined technology with design, data, or insights about human behavior, and what did they learn from it?

content.essay_identifier:
What excites you about a career in engineering/technology? Why do you think your abilities and skills are a good fit for
Plaksha's B.Tech program?
content.raw_text:
From a young age, I’ve been fascinated by how things work—whether it's the logic behind mobile apps, the science in smart devices, or the
innovation that powers everyday life. What excites me most about engineering and technology is the ability to solve real-world problems
using creativity and logic. It's not just about building machines or writing code—it's about designing solutions that can create meaningful
impact. The ever-evolving nature of tech, combined with its potential to transform industries from healthcare to climate, makes this field
incredibly exciting for me. I believe my curiosity, collaborative mindset, and eagerness to learn make me a strong fit for Plaksha’s B.Tech program. I enjoy working on
projects, asking questions, and applying what I learn to practical challenges. I also have a keen interest in interdisciplinary learning—blending
tech with design, data, and human behavior—which I understand is core to Plaksha’s approach. What draws me most to Plaksha is its focus
on innovation, entrepreneurship, and societal impact. I’m excited by the opportunity to learn from faculty, mentors, and peers who are
passionate about building a better future through technology. Plaksha’s hands-on curriculum, values-driven mission, and emphasis on
leadership resonate deeply with my aspirations.

signal.title: AI Excellence Isolation
signal.essay_claim: "I have a keen interest in interdisciplinary learning—blending tech with design, data, and human behavior."
signal.evidence_observation: In 10th grade the applicant earned a perfect score of 100 in the Artificial Intelligence subject (ACA-002), yet no AI‑related clubs, competitions, or projects appear in the activity profile.
signal.tension_or_coherence: TENSION — a perfect AI subject score indicates strong curricular engagement with AI, but the absence of any AI‑related extracurriculars suggests the interest may be confined to classroom performance.
signal.interview_hook: Beyond earning a perfect score in the AI subject, how has the applicant pursued AI learning or application independently, and what motivates them to continue studying AI after the course?

content.essay_identifier:
What excites you about a career in engineering/technology? Why do you think your abilities and skills are a good fit for
Plaksha's B.Tech program?
content.raw_text:
From a young age, I’ve been fascinated by how things work—whether it's the logic behind mobile apps, the science in smart devices, or the
innovation that powers everyday life. What excites me most about engineering and technology is the ability to solve real-world problems
using creativity and logic. It's not just about building machines or writing code—it's about designing solutions that can create meaningful
impact. The ever-evolving nature of tech, combined with its potential to transform industries from healthcare to climate, makes this field
incredibly exciting for me. I believe my curiosity, collaborative mindset, and eagerness to learn make me a strong fit for Plaksha’s B.Tech program. I enjoy working on
projects, asking questions, and applying what I learn to practical challenges. I also have a keen interest in interdisciplinary learning—blending
tech with design, data, and human behavior—which I understand is core to Plaksha’s approach. What draws me most to Plaksha is its focus
on innovation, entrepreneurship, and societal impact. I’m excited by the opportunity to learn from faculty, mentors, and peers who are
passionate about building a better future through technology. Plaksha’s hands-on curriculum, values-driven mission, and emphasis on
leadership resonate deeply with my aspirations.

signal.title: Empathy‑Driven Leadership Philosophy vs. Formal Roles
signal.essay_claim: "leadership is as much about listening and empathy as it is about action" and "perseverance—combined with teamwork and a clear sense of purpose—can gradually change mindsets."
signal.evidence_observation: Leadership activities include School House Captain (LEAD-005), Organization (LEAD-006), and Friends/Family (LEAD-007); none are explicitly linked to the stray dog project described in ESS-002 or demonstrate how listening and empathy were applied in those roles.
signal.tension_or_coherence: TENSION — the essay frames leadership as empathetic listening and perseverance, yet the listed leadership roles give no indication of how those qualities were exercised in formal positions.
signal.interview_hook: In the role of School House Captain, can you describe a situation where the applicant had to listen to opposing viewpoints and use empathy to reach a resolution, and what was the outcome?

content.essay_identifier:
Share a time when you contributed to a project or activity that positively affected your school or community. What
challenges did you face, and what did you learn about leadership and perseverance?
content.raw_text:
Having grown up with a pet dog, I’ve developed a deep sense of empathy and care for animals. That personal connection inspired me to take
action when I noticed several stray dogs in my neighborhood struggling with hunger, injuries, and neglect. I initiated a small project with a
few classmates to support these animals by organizing feeding drives, arranging basic medical aid through local NGOs, and spreading
awareness about responsible and humane treatment of strays. One of the major challenges was addressing concerns from some residents who were initially uncomfortable with our activities. There were
worries about safety and cleanliness. Instead of giving up, I took the time to understand their perspective and created a small awareness
campaign. We shared how caring for strays through sterilization and regular feeding could actually make the community safer. We also made
sure to clean up after every feeding and involved local animal welfare volunteers to lend credibility to our efforts. This experience taught me that leadership is as much about listening and empathy as it is about action. It showed me how
perseverance—combined with teamwork and a clear sense of purpose—can gradually change mindsets and make a difference. It also
reinforced that even small initiatives, when pursued with sincerity, can have a positive and lasting impact.

signal.title: Physics Performance Discrepancy Between School Scores and JEE Percentile
signal.essay_claim: "fascinated by how things work—whether it's the logic behind mobile apps, the science in smart devices, or the innovation that powers everyday life."
signal.evidence_observation: In 11th and 12th grades physics scores are 58 and 52 (ACA-003, ACA-004), markedly lower than other subjects, while the JEE Mains result shows a physics percentile of 96.67 and an overall score of 99.01 (TEST-001).
signal.tension_or_coherence: TENSION — school‑based physics performance drops sharply in the upper years, yet the applicant achieves a very high physics percentile on a national exam, indicating a mismatch between classroom assessment and aptitude test performance.
signal.interview_hook: When the applicant noticed their school physics grades lagging behind other subjects, what strategies did they use to improve their understanding, and how did they reconcile that effort with their strong JEE physics percentile?

6_llm_call_2_messages.content:
You are preparing an interviewer who has never met this applicant but has read
their application file. Your job is to produce interview themes and question
groups that will help the interviewer have a genuinely revealing conversation.

The interviewer has three specific goals for this interview:
  1. GRIT AND GROWTH — understanding how the applicant responds to setbacks,
     difficulty, and the gap between where they are and where they want to be.
  2. PROBLEM SOLVING — understanding how the applicant actually reasons through
     challenges, not just that they solved something.
  3. TECHNOLOGY ENGAGEMENT — understanding whether the applicant's interest in
     technology is genuine and self-directed, or primarily stated.

Every theme you produce must serve at least one of these three goals.
Every question group must contain at least one question that directly probes
the relevant goal through the specific evidence in the bundle.

---

YOUR INPUT:

You will receive a signal-evidence bundle. Each entry in signal_evidence_pairs
contains a signal and its supporting evidence.

The signal contains these fields:
- title: the name of the pattern or tension identified
- essay_claim: what the applicant claimed or implied in their essay
- evidence_observation: what the activity or academic data actually shows
- tension_or_coherence: whether these align or conflict, and how
- interview_hook: the specific line of inquiry an interviewer needs to pursue

The interview_hook is the most important field. It tells you exactly what the
interviewer needs to understand that the application cannot answer by itself.
Your questions must address the interview_hook directly and specifically.

---

HOW TO BUILD THEMES:

Do not create one theme per signal mechanically. Read all signals together and
group them by which interviewer goal they serve. Signals that both relate to
the applicant's academic response to challenge should become one theme, not two.
Aim for 3 to 4 themes that each represent a meaningful interview territory.

Each theme must:
- Have a title that names something specific to this applicant, not a generic
  category. "Academic Resilience Amid Board Transition" is specific.
  "Academic Performance" is not.
- Have a description that explains what the interviewer is trying to understand
  about this person, not just what the evidence shows.
- Reference the entity IDs of the evidence that supports it.

---

HOW TO BUILD QUESTIONS:

For each theme, produce 3 to 4 questions. At least one must directly address
the interview_hook of the signal that drove the theme. The others should probe
the same territory from different angles.

A question is only acceptable if it meets both of these tests:
  TEST 1 — Could this question only be asked about this specific applicant?
           If the same question could be asked of any applicant in this field,
           it fails. Rewrite it to name something specific.
  TEST 2 — Does this question probe reasoning or motivation rather than asking
           for elaboration? Questions of the form "tell me more about X" or
           "can you elaborate on X" fail. Questions that ask why a specific
           choice was made, how the applicant thought through a specific
           situation, or what the relationship is between two specific things
           they did pass.

PROHIBITED QUESTION FORMS:
- "Tell me about X"
- "Can you elaborate on X"
- "How did your interest in X develop over time"
- "Can you walk me through X"
- Any question that does not name something specific from this application

---

CONTRAST EXAMPLE — understand the difference:

Signal context: applicant's essay emphasizes excitement about solving real-world
problems through technology, but activity profile shows piano, yoga, olympiads,
and reading — no tech projects or internships.
Interview hook: what concrete steps has the applicant taken to build or create
technology beyond coursework, and how do they plan to close the gap between
stated ambition and demonstrated technical work.

WRONG question:
"How has your interest in technology developed over time?"

RIGHT question:
"Your essay describes excitement about designing solutions that create meaningful
impact, but your activities outside school are piano, yoga, and reading — where
in your life have you actually built or created something technical, even
informally, and what happened?"

The right question could not have been asked without reading this specific
application. It names the specific tension. It does not ask for elaboration —
it forces the applicant to account for a gap.

---

PROHIBITED TERMS: Admit, Reject, Likelihood, Top candidate, Risk factor, Strength, Weakness, Outstanding, Exceptional, Excellent, Poor, Impressive, Concerning

---

OUTPUT SCHEMA — return exactly this structure, nothing else:

{
  "themes": [
    {
      "theme_id": "THEME-###",
      "title": "Specific label naming something particular to this applicant",
      "description": "What the interviewer is trying to understand about this person through this theme",
      "referenced_entity_ids": ["Entity IDs that support this theme"]
    }
  ],
  "question_groups": [
    {
      "theme_id": "THEME-###",
      "group_title": "Short neutral label for the question group",
      "questions": [
        "Question 1 — specific, probing, names something from this application",
        "Question 2 — probes from a different angle",
        "Question 3 — addresses the interview_hook directly"
      ]
    }
  ]
}

Theme IDs numbered sequentially from THEME-001.
Every question_group must reference a theme_id defined in themes.
questions must be a flat array of plain strings only.
Produce one question_group per theme.

themes.title: Bridging Tech Ambition with Hands‑On Project Experience
themes.description: Understand what concrete steps the applicant has taken outside class to design or build technology‑based solutions, how they pursue AI learning beyond a perfect score, and how they combine technology with design, data, or human behavior in practice.

themes.title: Empathetic Leadership in Action: From Stray Dogs to School House
themes.description: Explore how the applicant’s philosophy of leadership as listening and empathy translates into their formal roles, especially as School House Captain, and what they learned from navigating opposition in community initiatives.

themes.title: Navigating the Gap Between Classroom Physics Grades and JEE Aptitude
themes.description: Understand how the applicant responded when their school physics performance lagged, what strategies they used to improve, and how they reconcile that effort with their high JEE physics percentile, revealing their approach to overcoming academic setbacks.

questions: You wrote that you enjoy working on projects and applying what you learn to practical challenges, yet your activity list includes piano, yoga, Olympiads, and reading with no tech projects. Can you name a specific instance outside of school where you designed or built something technological—even a small prototype or script—and explain how you decided it counted as a project?

You earned a perfect 100 in the AI subject in 10th grade, but no AI‑related clubs or projects appear in your activities. What have you done on your own to explore AI beyond the curriculum, and what kept you motivated to continue learning after the course?

Your essay mentions blending tech with design, data, and human behavior, yet your activities are largely solitary like reading and music. Describe a concrete experience—such as a project, competition, or collaboration—where you intentionally combined technology with one of those domains, and what you learned from it.

As School House Captain, tell me about a specific time you had to listen to residents who opposed your stray‑dog feeding initiative and used empathy to address their concerns—what did you learn from that exchange?

Your essay says leadership is as much about listening and empathy as about action, yet your listed leadership roles (House Captain, Organization, Friends/Family) don’t show how you applied those qualities. Choose one of those roles and explain a moment where you deliberately practiced listening before acting, and why you chose that approach.
In the stray‑dog project, you mentioned changing mindsets of skeptical residents. What specific steps did you take to shift their perspective, and how did you measure whether your effort was working?

You scored 58 in 11th‑grade physics and 52 in 12th‑grade physics, yet your JEE Mains physics percentile is 96.67. When you first saw those school scores drop, what specific steps did you take to improve your grasp of physics, and how did you align that work with your JEE preparation?
Your overall school average remained high despite low physics scores. How did you decide where to allocate your study time between physics and other subjects, and what trade‑offs did you consider?
Looking back, what did the experience of low classroom physics grades teach you about how you learn best, and how has that influenced your approach to challenging subjects since?

essays.prompt:
What excites you about a career in engineering/technology? Why do you think your abilities and skills are a good fit for
Plaksha's B.Tech program?
essays.full_text:
From a young age, I’ve been fascinated by how things work—whether it's the logic behind mobile apps, the science in smart devices, or the
innovation that powers everyday life. What excites me most about engineering and technology is the ability to solve real-world problems
using creativity and logic. It's not just about building machines or writing code—it's about designing solutions that can create meaningful
impact. The ever-evolving nature of tech, combined with its potential to transform industries from healthcare to climate, makes this field
incredibly exciting for me. I believe my curiosity, collaborative mindset, and eagerness to learn make me a strong fit for Plaksha’s B.Tech program. I enjoy working on
projects, asking questions, and applying what I learn to practical challenges. I also have a keen interest in interdisciplinary learning—blending
tech with design, data, and human behavior—which I understand is core to Plaksha’s approach. What draws me most to Plaksha is its focus
on innovation, entrepreneurship, and societal impact. I’m excited by the opportunity to learn from faculty, mentors, and peers who are
passionate about building a better future through technology. Plaksha’s hands-on curriculum, values-driven mission, and emphasis on
leadership resonate deeply with my aspirations.

essays.prompt:
Share a time when you contributed to a project or activity that positively affected your school or community. What
challenges did you face, and what did you learn about leadership and perseverance?
essays.full_text:
Having grown up with a pet dog, I’ve developed a deep sense of empathy and care for animals. That personal connection inspired me to take
action when I noticed several stray dogs in my neighborhood struggling with hunger, injuries, and neglect. I initiated a small project with a
few classmates to support these animals by organizing feeding drives, arranging basic medical aid through local NGOs, and spreading
awareness about responsible and humane treatment of strays. One of the major challenges was addressing concerns from some residents who were initially uncomfortable with our activities. There were
worries about safety and cleanliness. Instead of giving up, I took the time to understand their perspective and created a small awareness
campaign. We shared how caring for strays through sterilization and regular feeding could actually make the community safer. We also made
sure to clean up after every feeding and involved local animal welfare volunteers to lend credibility to our efforts. This experience taught me that leadership is as much about listening and empathy as it is about action. It showed me how
perseverance—combined with teamwork and a clear sense of purpose—can gradually change mindsets and make a difference. It also
reinforced that even small initiatives, when pursued with sincerity, can have a positive and lasting impact.

interpreted_signals.tension_or_coherence: TENSION — the essay claims an interdisciplinary blend of tech with design, data, and human behavior, but the activities listed are largely solitary (reading, music, yoga) with no evident design, data, or human‑behavior projects.
interpreted_signals.interview_hook: Can you point to a concrete experience—such as a project, competition, or collaboration—where the applicant intentionally combined technology with design, data, or insights about human behavior, and what did they learn from it?
